title: What Is the Cloud?
subtitle: It's not as mysterious as it sounds

sections.content:
The **cloud** is one of those words that sounds magical but is surprisingly simple. It just means **using someone else's computers over the internet** instead of running everything on your own machine.

Before the cloud, if you wanted to run a website, you had to buy a physical server, set it up in a room, keep it cool, fix it when it broke, and replace it when it got old. That's expensive and complicated.

With the cloud, you rent computing power from companies like Amazon (AWS), Google (Google Cloud), or Microsoft (Azure). They own the servers, keep them running, and you just pay for what you use.
sections.analogy: The cloud is like renting an apartment instead of buying a house. When you own a house, you handle everything — maintenance, repairs, lawn care. When you rent, the landlord handles all of that. You just use the space and pay monthly. Cloud computing works the same way: you use the servers and pay for what you need, while the cloud provider handles all the infrastructure.
keyPoints: "The cloud" = other people's computers that you rent over the internet
Major cloud providers: AWS (Amazon), Google Cloud, Microsoft Azure
You pay for what you use — no need to buy expensive hardware
Almost every app and website you use runs on the cloud

sections.instruction: Here's how the cloud works in practice, step by step.

steps.title: 1. You write code locally
steps.description: You write your app's code on your own laptop using a code editor. At this point, only you can use it — it's just files on your machine.
steps.highlight: Your laptop is where development happens

steps.title: 2. Upload to a cloud provider
steps.description: When you're ready to share your app with the world, you upload your code to a cloud provider like AWS, Google Cloud, or Vercel. This is called "deploying."
steps.highlight: Deploying = sending your code to the cloud

steps.title: 3. Cloud runs your code on their servers
steps.description: The cloud provider takes your code and runs it on their powerful servers in massive data centers around the world. They handle power, cooling, security, and backups.
steps.highlight: Data centers are warehouses full of servers

steps.title: 4. Users access it via the internet
steps.description: Now anyone with a web browser can visit your app. When they do, they're actually connecting to the cloud provider's servers — not your laptop. Your app is live 24/7 even when your laptop is off.
steps.highlight: Your laptop can be closed — the cloud keeps running

sections.question: What does "the cloud" actually mean?
options: Other people's computers that you rent over the internet
sections.explanation: The cloud is not magical or mysterious — it's just computers owned by companies like Amazon, Google, and Microsoft that you can rent. When someone says their app "runs in the cloud," it means it runs on servers in a data center somewhere, not on their own machine.

sections.prompt: The cloud is basically other people's _____.

completionMessage: You now understand the cloud — it's just rented computing power. No magic, no mystery. Companies like AWS and Google own massive data centers, and developers rent space on those servers to run their apps. Next, we'll learn how code gets from your laptop to the cloud.
